explicit_text: I was born in Kumamoto on February 28, and my family lived there for many years. My father was a farmer who grew rice on inherited land, though the amount decreased over time. Kumamoto was also the place where the Ten Year War affected many families, including mine, as many relatives fought in battles like Tabaruzaka and Kanakugi. I worked and studied there before deciding to leave for America.
implicit_text: I was born in the city on February 28, and my family lived there for many years. My father was a farmer who grew rice on inherited land, though the amount decreased over time. That city was also the place where the Ten Year War affected many families, including mine, as many relatives fought in battles like Tabaruzaka and Kanakugi. I worked and studied there before deciding to leave for America.

explicit_text: I only heard about the Ten Year War from older people in Kumamoto. They told me the battles at Tabaruzaka and Kanakugi were fierce and many people died. Four brothers of my relative went to fight with Saigō Takamori, but only one returned alive. The war had a lasting impact on the community and my family’s history.
implicit_text: I only heard about the decade-long conflict from older people in my hometown. They told me the battles at Tabaruzaka and Kanakugi were fierce and many people died. explicit_text: Tabaruzaka was one of the fierce battle sites during the Ten Year War that I heard about from elders in Kumamoto. Many people died there, including relatives of mine who fought alongside Saigō Takamori. It was a significant place tied to the struggles of that era and the sacrifices made by my family and community.

explicit_text: Kanakugi was another battlefield during the Ten Year War that I learned about from older people in Kumamoto. Like Tabaruzaka, it was a place of fierce fighting and many casualties. My relatives who joined Saigō Takamori’s forces fought there, marking it as a place of family and local history.

explicit_text: Saigō Takamori was a revered leader whom many in Kumamoto, including my relatives, supported during the Ten Year War. Four brothers of my relative fought under him, though only one survived. Everyone seemed to like Saigō, and he led many soldiers from Kumamoto with swords and rifles. Even my wife’s father went to war with him when he was just 16 years old.

Even my wife’s father went to war with him when he was just 16 years old.

explicit_text: I left Japan from Kobe on an old British ship in November 1899 to come to America. The ship did not stop there, but it was the port from which we sailed. From Kobe, we went to Yokohama before heading overseas. It was the starting point of my journey to a new life.
implicit_text: I left Japan from a major port city on an old British ship in November 1899 to come to America. The ship did not stop there, but it was the port from which we sailed. From that city, we went to Yokohama before heading overseas. It was the starting point of my journey to a new life.

explicit_text: Our ship did not stop at Tokyo during my voyage from Japan to America. Instead, it stopped at Yokohama for two or three nights, where we went ashore for dinner. I did not have any direct experiences in Tokyo during that time, but it was part of the route we bypassed.

explicit_text: The ship I took from Japan stopped at Yokohama for two or three nights during the journey to America. We went ashore there to have dinner, which was a welcome break. Yokohama was an important port on my route from Kobe to Canada and then to the United States.
implicit_text: The ship I took from Japan stopped at a major port near the capital for two or three nights during the journey to America. We went ashore there to have dinner, which was a welcome break. That port was an important stop on my route from the departure city to Canada and then to the United States.

explicit_text: I landed in Canada first when I came to America in 1899. I stayed there about a week, but it was too cold for me, so I moved on to Sacramento by train. The British consul in Canada offered me land and financial help, but I didn’t know how to take advantage of it. People from a Japanese hotel in Victoria helped me get settled, and I was surprised how kind they were.

People from a Japanese hotel in Victoria helped me get settled, and I was surprised how kind they were.

explicit_text: After arriving in Canada, I took a train to Sacramento, California, where I stayed near a man from Kumamoto who owned a barber shop. The journey was difficult because we didn’t know English, and we struggled to eat until a kind American helped us. People from Hiroshima were already there, and some who came on the same ship had settled in Sacramento before me.
implicit_text: After arriving in the neighboring country, I took a train to a city in California, where I stayed near a man from my home region who owned a barber shop. The journey was difficult because we didn’t know English, and we struggled to eat until a kind American helped us. People from Hiroshima were already there, and some who came on the same ship had settled in that city before me.

explicit_text: In Canada, I stayed briefly in Victoria, British Columbia, where there was only one Japanese hotel run by a man named Ozawa. The city was small then, but when I visited many years later, it had grown significantly. The Japanese community there was small but supportive, and many Japanese worked at the local sawmill.

explicit_text: In Sacramento and nearby areas, I met many people from Hiroshima, including Mr. Hirokawa who lived on the other side of Santa Maria. There was also Mr. Tanaka from Hiroshima who worked at Buna, though he was gone when I returned. The Hiroshima community was part of the Japanese immigrant network I encountered in California.

explicit_text: I lived and worked in Santa Maria, California, growing sugar beets and making friends with many Japanese, especially from Kagoshima. Life there was good; most of us were single young men who enjoyed socializing by gambling or playing billiards after work. I stayed there around age 26 or 27 and found the local white people friendly and helpful.

explicit_text: I lived in Guadalupe, California for about seven years working in sugar beet farming under Mr. Hagiya. It was my first place after arriving in America, and I stayed there before moving to Santa Maria. The work was hard but steady, and I was part of a Japanese farming community in the area.

explicit_text: In Santa Maria, I made friends with a few people from Kagoshima Prefecture who worked seasonally in the sugar beet fields. Although I was from Kumamoto, the Kagoshima men were welcoming and often invited me to dinner. Their presence helped me feel connected to other Japanese immigrants from southern Japan.

However, I was inexperienced and didn’t know how to make use of that assistance. The British Empire’s presence was part of my early experience immigrating to North America.

explicit_text: On the ship to Canada, I met a man named Tamekichi Nishiyama who was working as a policeman in Canada. Later, a friend in Tacoma told me Nishiyama was from the same village as me, which was a surprising connection. I only remembered his name from that journey, but it was meaningful to know someone from home was there.

explicit_text: Rev. Futaba was a Buddhist priest who took me to the cemetery in Santa Maria when I visited two years ago. I was surprised to find about ten of my friends buried there, which was a poignant reminder of the community we had built. He helped me connect with the memories of those who had passed away.
